coredata.dc:title: A label-free aptasensor for ultrasensitive Pb2+ detection based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between carbon nitride nanofibers and Ru(phen)3 2+
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Yujiao
Li, Yang
Li, Lingling
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: A label-free aptasensor was developed for ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) from graphitic carbon nitride nanofibers (CNNFs) to Ru(phen)3 2+. The CNNFs synthesized via a facile two-step hydrolysis-electrolysis strategy showed intense and stable ECL signal by taking advantages of amplifying and stabilizing effect of carbon nanotubes and Au nanoparticles. After the specific hybridation between capture DNA and Pb2+ specific aptamer, Ru(phen)3 2+ could be captured onto CNNFs modified electrode by effectively intercalating into the grooves of double-strand DNA, thus triggering the ECL-RET and leading to highly enhanced ECL intensity. The presence of Pb2+ would result in the detachment of Ru(phen)3 2+ and then the inhibition of ECL-RET. Then Pb2+ concentration could be quantified based on ECL change before and after introduction of Pb2+. The target recycling based on exonuclease I (Exo I) mediated digestion of Pb2+-aptamer complex was implemented to further improve the sensitivity. These synergistic amplification strategies enabled the aptasensor to be ultrasensitive for Pb2+ determination with a detection limit of 0.04 pM. The proposed probe was utilized to analyze environmental samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Lead detection
Electrochemiluminescence
Resonance energy transfer
Exonuclease-assisted target recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL behaviors of CNNFs and PCNTs-AuNPs
Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (A) two-step strategy for preparation of CNNFs and (B) fabrication of the proposed aptasensor for Pb2+ detection.

(A) TEM image of H-CN. (B) TEM image of CNNFs. (C) AFM image of CNNFs and the corresponding section analysis (D) and height distribution (E). (F) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CNNF dispersion. (G) FT-IR spectra of bulk CN and CNNFs.
Corresponding CV, ECL signal responses, and stability test (inset) of (A) CNNFs/GCE and (B) AuNPs/CNNFs /GCE, respectively.

(A) PL spectra of (a) CNNFs and (b) Ru(phen)3 2+, (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ru(phen)3 2+. (B) ECL responses of (a) CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE, (b) cDNA/CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE, (c) MCH/cDNA/CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE, (d) aptamer/MCH/cDNA/CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE, Ru(phen)3 2+/aptamer/MCH/cDNA/CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE (e) before and (f) after introduction of Pb2+ and Exo I, (g) Ru(phen)3 2+/aptamer/MCH/cDNA/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE. (C) ECL spectrum of Ru(phen)3 2+/aptamer/MCH/cDNA/CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs/GCE.

(A) ECL responses of the aptasensor in the presence of CEA standards with various concentrations. (B) Calibration curve for Pb2+ determination. (C) Selectivity of the aptasensor for Pb2+. (D) Stability of the aptasensor in the presence of 1 nM Pb2+.
Comparison of main methods reported for the determination of Pb2+.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: DPV-Differential pulse voltammetry; CHA-Catalytic hairpin assembly; CA-Chronoamperometric; MOF-Metal-organic framework; EIS-Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; OMC-Ordered mesoporous carbon; PEC-Photoelectrochemical.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Pb2+ in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A label-free aptasensor for ultrasensitive Pb2+ detection based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between carbon nitride nanofibers and Ru(phen)3 2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yujiao Peng and Yang Li contributed equally to this work.
Permanent address: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free aptasensor was developed for ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) from graphitic carbon nitride nanofibers (CNNFs) to Ru(phen)3 2+. The CNNFs synthesized via a facile two-step hydrolysis-electrolysis strategy showed intense and stable ECL signal by taking advantages of amplifying and stabilizing effect of carbon nanotubes and Au nanoparticles. After the specific hybridation between capture DNA and Pb2+ specific aptamer, Ru(phen)3 2+ could be captured onto CNNFs modified electrode by effectively intercalating into the grooves of double-strand DNA, thus triggering the ECL-RET and leading to highly enhanced ECL intensity. The presence of Pb2+ would result in the detachment of Ru(phen)3 2+ and then the inhibition of ECL-RET. Then Pb2+ concentration could be quantified based on ECL change before and after introduction of Pb2+. The target recycling based on exonuclease I (Exo I) mediated digestion of Pb2+-aptamer complex was implemented to further improve the sensitivity. These synergistic amplification strategies enabled the aptasensor to be ultrasensitive for Pb2+ determination with a detection limit of 0.04 pM. The proposed probe was utilized to analyze environmental samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: As a typical heavy-metal pollutant, lead ion (Pb2+) can cause neurological, cardio-vascular, reproductive, and developmental disorders upon a little amount of exposure [1]. Thus, the sensitive detection of Pb2+ at trace or ultra-trace level is of paramount importance. Although the traditional techniques for Pb2+ determination, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), are sufficiently sensitive, they are costly, complicated, labor intensive and time-consuming, thus limiting their wide application [2]. Alternatively, a large amount of sensitive, rapid and inexpensive biosensors has been designed for Pb2+ analysis on the basis of electrochemistry [3,4], photoluminescence (PL) [5], photoelectrochemical [6], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [7] and so on. Among them, ECL is considered as a promising and powerful analytical technique thanks to its unique advantages including near-zero background, high sensitivity, and simple setup [8]. Since the report on ECL of Si nanocrystals [9], nanomaterials as ECL luminophores have driven the development of sensing strategies and boosted the sensing performance. Thus, the exploration of novel ECL-active nanomaterials has recently attracted considerable attention.

As an emerging nanomaterial, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) with p-conjugated planar topography formed by sp2 bonded carbon and nitrogen has attracted tremendous attentions in the fields ranging from photocatalysis or electrocatalysis to optical sensors. CN was also demonstrated as a good candidate with high ECL efficiency and has been applied for the construction of various ECL biosensors [10–12]. However, the present works on ECL of CN mostly used the pristine bulk powder or CN nanosheets (CNNSs) via ultrasonication or acid oxidation assisted exfoliation [13,14]. CN nanofibers (CNNFs), as the one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure of CN, possess unique properties as compared to 2D CNNSs, such as higher surface to volume ratio and more active sites. However, the synthesis of CNNFs is still under the initial stage, and those methods previously reported for preparation of CNNFs required harsh reaction conditions such as the usage of template, high temperature polymerization, and concentrated acid or alkaline which might inevitably damage the crystal structure [15–19]. Moreover, the sensing application of CNNFs has been seldom explored, let alone the ECL application. In this regard, it is very promising to develop facile synthetic method for CNNFs with minimized damage and explore CNNFs as alternative ECL luminophors in the analysis.

In addition to using nanomaterials as ECL luminophores, appropriate sensing strategy has played an important role in improving the analysis performance. ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) has been a promising strategy for the construction of sensitive biosensors [20,21]. The overlap between the ECL spectra of donors and the absorption spectra of acceptors is vital for ECL-RET. CNNSs have been playing growing role as donor in ECL-RET based biosensors, thus it is eagerly expected to explore the promising role of CNNFs with different morphology and chemical structure in the ECL-RET system. On the other hand, as a conventional ECL reagent, the Ru(bpy)3 2+ complex has been widely used in ECL sensors as sole ECL luminophore, or as either donor or acceptor in ECL-RET system. Despite the advantages and achievement of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in ECL system, its application, to some extent, is limited by the necessity of tedious labelling process and strong background light in alkaline condition [22,23]. Compared to Ru(bpy)3 2+, Ru(phen)3 2+ (Fig. S1) has better adsorption capacity, larger molecular scaffolds and high ECL emission efficiency [23,24]. More importantly, Ru(phen)3 2+ can specifically intercalate into the grooves of double strand DNA (ds-DNA), thus avoiding the extra labelling process [22,25]. Therefore, Ru(phen)3 2+ is promising alternative to Ru(bpy)3 2+. However, it has been rarely used to construct ECL sensors, especially in ECL-RET based sensors.

In view of above issues, a sensitive label-free aptasensor for Pb2+ detection was constructed based on ECL-RET between CNNFs and Ru(phen)3 2+ (Scheme 1 ). A novel two-step hydrolysis-electrolysis method was proposed for synthesis of CNNFs with preserved crystal structure and prominent PL and ECL properties. The composite (PCNTs-AuNPs) composed of poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) functionalized carbon nanotubes and Au nanoparticles were used to modify electrode before capturing CNNFs, which could effectively suppress the electrode passivation encountered by CNNFs, giving substantially stable ECL signal. Based on the effective intercalation of Ru(phen)3 2+ in to the grooves of hybridized complementary DNA-aptamer, Ru(phen)3 2+ could be excited by the energy from CNNFs, leading to highly enhanced ECL intensity. The presence of Pb2+ would lead to the release of Ru(phen)3 2+ from electrode owing to the formation of Pb2+-aptamer complex. To further amplify the signal, the exonuclease-assisted target recycling was introduced via selective digestion of Pb2+-aptamer complex from the 3′-end of the aptamer to release Pb2+ again for recycling and signal amplification [26–28]. Profiting from the triple amplification with PCNTs-AuNPs, ECL-RET and exonuclease-assisted target recycling, respectively, the developed aptasensor was ultrasensitive for Pb2+determination with a detection limit of 0.04 pM.

CNNFs were synthesized by alkaline hydrolysis combined with a simple electrochemical process from bulk graphitic carbon nitride (CN) for the first time (see Experimental section in Supplementary Information). Two control samples via separately hydrolyzing or electrolyzing the bulk CN under the same conditions were fabricated and denoted as H-CN and E-CN, respectively.

5 μL of PCNTs-AuNPs (see Supplementary Information) was spread on the cleaned glassy carbon working electrode (GCE) firstly and dried at the room temperature. Afterward, 5 μL of CNNFs was introduced to the surface of the electrode to fabricate the ECL sensor platform. Next, 5 μL of 2 μM complementary strand (cDNA) was dropped on the electrode surface overnight at 4 °C. After being rinsed by double-distilled water, 1.0 mM of 6-Mercaptohexanol (MCH) was added on the sensing surface for 1 h to block the remaining binding sites. Then, 5 μL of 2 μM aptamer was incubated for 2 h on the GCE surface at 37 °C. Ultimately, the above electrode was further reacted with 5 μL of 5 mM Ru(phen)3 2+ for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, the obtained electrode was kept at 4 °C for further use.

For Pb2+ detection, the constructed sensor (see Experimental section in Supplementary Information) was incubated with Pb2+ solution with different concentrations containing 20 U Exo I for 1 h at 37 °C. Lastly, after thorough washing, the obtained aptasensor was put in 0.1 M pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 and 0.1 M KCl to detect signal from 0 to −1.2 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.

The schematic of the two-step method for the synthesis of CNNFs is described in Scheme 1A. The bulk CN, as the starting materials, was first hydrolyzed at low temperature (60 °C) in 2 M NaOH solution, and the intermediate product H-CN was then electrolyzed under 10 V (DC) to obtain the final CNNFs. The morphology evolution of the bulk CN during the two-step hydrolysis-hydrolysis process was studied by transmission electron micrographs (TEM). The pristine bulk CN mostly consisted of micro-sized aggregated particles (Fig. S2), while the obtained H-CN after hydrolyzing in alkaline solution showed a thin porous sheet-like structure (Fig. 1 A), indicating that the hydrolysis treatment could not only exfoliate the bulk CN but also create pores on the basal plane. It was inferred that the hydroxide and water molecules could intercalate into the interplanar spacing, leading to weakened van der Waals force between CN layers and enlarged layer spacing. Thus, the bulk parent CN materials could be readily exfoliated into few-layered nanosheets with the assistance of followed ultrasonication treatment. On the other hand, it was reported that the unstable domains of CN could be eliminated via decomposition in alkaline solution to form mesoporous structure [29]. The presence of defect pores made the CN sheets somewhat fragile and vulnerable to chemical attack from OH− ions. As a result, large CN sheets were be scissored into smaller units with functional groups such as -NHx and -OH [15,30].

It is noteworthy that, in order to protect the unique structure of CN from damage in harsh synthesis process, the alkaline hydrolysis was operated under milder condition than other reports in which concentrated NaOH or KOH solution and high temperature were used for activation and “top-down” synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials [15,30,31]. The treated CN therefore was partially cleaved, and further electrolysis treatment was carried out to realize complete cutting. The applied potential here was high enough to drive oxidize water to form active hydroxyl and oxygen radicals. These radicals, together with OH− ions, could attack physical and/or chemical defects of porous H-CN to initiate chemical scissoring process, leading to further breakage of H-CN into CNNFs [32,33]. TEM images of CNNFs shown in Fig. 1B clearly indicated homogeneous fiber-like nanostructures with an average width of 10 nm and a length of several micrometers. Besides, the nearly transparent feature of CNNFs (Fig. 1B, inset) indicated their ultrathin thickness, which was confirmed by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) test (Fig. 1C–E) to be in the range of 1–5 nm with an average thickness of 2.2 nm.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized CNNFs were characterized by the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 1F). The CNNFs exhibited a strong absorption peak at 217 nm with a shoulder at 286 nm, as well as blue-shifted maximum emission peak at 420 nm in comparison with bulk CN, which coincided with previous literatures [30,34]. The chemical structure of as-obtained CNNFs was investigated by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1G). Obviously, the FT-IR spectra of CNNFs resembled that of bulk CN, both showing the characteristic breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units at 810 cm−1 and typical stretching vibration modes of CN and CN heterocycles between 1000–1700 cm-1 [35,36]. These results indicated that CNNFs kept the molecular structure of tri-s-triazine in the parent bulk CN after two-step hydrolysis-electrolysis treatment. Notably, the broad peaks between 3000–3500 cm-1 originating from the -NHx and -OH stretching vibration modes were much stronger in comparion with that of bulk CN, suggesting the appearance of these functional groups in synthesis process [33].

To get deep insight into the proposed synthesis method, the optical properties and FT-IR spectra of H-CN and E-CN were also investigated for comparison (Fig. S4-5). Remarkably, the PL quantum yields were calculated to be 32.86% for CNNFs, 13.56% for H-CN, and 6.49% for E-CN, respectively. These results revealed that the successive hydrolysis and electrolysis could exert synergistic effect on the production of desirable CNNFs with superior PL, which was impossible to be obtained by them independently.

Furthermore, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted to identify phase and crystal structure of CNNFs as shown in Fig. S6. The (100) peak at 13° and (002) peak at 27° were assigned to the in-plane repeated tri-s-triazine units and the interlayer stacking reflection, respectively [36]. These two peaks were both significantly decreased in CNNFs, suggesting the successful exfoliation and breakage of bulk CN [37]. It should be noted, the visible (100) peak was indicative of the preserved tri-s-triazine units in as-obtained CNNFs, which was undetectable for most reported CN nanofibers due to structural deformation [16,19,38]. All these observations confirmed that the proposed mild synthetic method could reach a delicate balance between successful disintegration of bulk CN and minimized structural damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of CNNFs and PCNTs-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: ECL and cyclic voltammetry (CV) behaviors of the as-prepared CNNFs were investigated with K2S2O8 as the co-reactant. As shown in Fig. 2 A, when the potential was scanned from 0 to −1.5 V, the CNNFs-modified electrode exhibited an obvious cathodic peak at around −1.0 V, which was attributed to the electrochemical reduction of S2O8 2−. Meanwhile, the CNNFs-modified electrode gave a strong ECL emission onset and maximum potential at −1.02 and −1.28 V, respectively, which was in line with previous report [39,40]. Notably, the CNNFs showed significantly stronger ECL emission and lower peak potential than H-CN and E-CN (Fig. S7), which was identical to the PL behavior, further indicating that the as-prepared CNNFs were promising for ECL application.

The strong ECL emission of CNNFs was proposed as follows. Upon the potential scanning towards negative direction, the CNNF and S2O8 2− were reduced to CNNF•- and SO4 •-radicals, respectively. Alternatively, the strong oxidant SO4 •- might oxidize CNNF to CNNF+. Subsequently, CNNF•- would react with SO4 •- or CNNF+ to produce excited state CNNF*, which decayed back to the ground state with light emission [41].

However, the apparent degradation of the ECL emission of CNNFs-modified electrode in the potential range of 0 to −1.5 V (Fig. 2A, inset) would limit its application. Such instable ECL signal resulted from electrode passivation induced by over-injection of highly energetic electrons into the conduction bands of CNNFs at large cathodic potential [42]. This problem was resolved mostly by appropriately functionalizing the CN plane with metal nanomaterials [42,43]. Aiming to eliminate the electrode passivation, PCNTs-AuNPs nanocomposites (Fig. S8), comprising electrostatically interacted PDDA functionalized CNTs and AuNPs, were used to modify GCE before capturing CNNFs. ECL and CV behaviors of the PCNTs-AuNPs/CNNFs modified electrode were depicted in Fig. 2B. In contrast to CNNFs, the PCNTs-AuNPs/CNNFs modified electrode gave more positive reduction potential of S2O8 2− (−0.75 V), much lower ECL onset potential (−0.85 V) and peak potential (−1.10 V). Moreover, the electrochemical current and ECL intensity were significantly stronger than that of CNNFs modified electrode. This enormously boosted behavior could be attributed to the increasing surface area of modified electrode (Fig. S9), electrocatalytic reduction of S2O8 2− by AuNPs, as well as excellent electronic transmission ability of well conductive AuNPs and CNTs.

More importantly, the ECL stability of PCNTs-AuNPs/CNNFs was significantly improved as compared to CNNFs. It was obvious that ECL intensity of the PCNTs-AuNPs/CNNFs film showed no sign of attenuation over time at the potential window of 0 to −1.2 V (Fig. 2B, inset). It was envisaged that CNTs and AuNPs positively affected the ECL stability of modified electrode thanks to their excellent conductivity and catalysis effect (discussion in Supplementary Information).

As illustrated in Scheme 1, a novel aptasensor for Pb2+ detection was designed based on ECL-RET between CNNFs and Ru(phen)3 2+. After the PCNTs-AuNPs and CNNFs were modified onto the electrode in order, cDNA with a thiol at its 5′ end was immobilized on the modified electrode via Au-S bond, and the modified electrode surface was further blocked with MCH. Then the Pb2+-specific aptamer would hybridize with cDNA on the electrode to form ds-DNA structure. After the intercalation of Ru(phen)3 2+ in the grooves of hybridized DNA, the ECL intensity changed due to ECL-RET from CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+. Afterwards, in the presence of Pb2+ and Exo I, the specific recognition of the aptamer to Pb2+ led to the dissociation of the ds-DNA and release of intercalated Ru(phen)3 2+ because of the higher stability of the aptamer-Pb2+ complex. As a result, the ECL-RET was inhibited and the ECL intensity was recovered. Meanwhile, Exo I selectively digested the aptamer-Pb2+ complex from the 3′- termini of the aptamer to release Pb2+, which would bind with rest aptamer, achieving the analyte recycling. The difference in ECL intensity before and after the introduction of Pb2+ and Exo I was used to quantify Pb2+. The successful stepwise electrode modification of the aptasensor was demonstrated by EIS using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as electroactive probes (Fig. S11).

To verify the feasibility of ECL-RET from CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+, the spectral overlap between emission spectrum of CNNFs and absorption spectrum of Ru(phen)3 2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, Ru(phen)3 2+ had intense excitonic absorption in the range of 400–450 nm, which matched well with the PL spectrum of CNNFs (420 nm). Accordingly, the considerable spectral overlap ensured efficient ECL-RET from CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+.

The ECL responses of different modified electrodes after each step were recorded as shown in Fig. 3B. The successive modification of cDNA, MCH, and aptamer resulted in the gradual decreases in ECL intensity (curve b-d). However, after the intercalation of Ru(phen)3 2+, significantly enhanced ECL was observed (curve e). Since Ru(phen)3 2+ had negligible cathodic ECL emission in the potential range of 0 to −1.2 V (curve g), the ECL enhancement might be caused by ECL-RET from CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+. Furthermore, the ECL intensity declined remarkably upon the introduction of Pb2+ and Exo I owing to the release of and Ru(phen)3 2+ and termination of ECL-RET (curve f). Moreover, in the absence of Ru(phen)3 2+, Pb2+ had no direct influence on the ECL intensity of CNNFs/PCNTs-AuNPs modified electrode (Fig. S12), further verifying that the change in ECL response of the proposed aptasensor was indeed caused by ECL-RET.

To further verify the possibility of signal enhancement by ECL-RET, the ECL spectrum of the electrode after Ru(phen)3 2+ intercalation was recorded using a series of optical filters (Fig. 3C). Two peaks centered at 430 and 577 nm could be assigned to emission from CNNFs and Ru(phen)3 2+, respectively, indicating the efficient ECL-RET from emissive CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+. More importantly, the emission from Ru(phen)3 2+ was remarkably stronger than that from CNNFs. This was because the transferred energy from CNNFs could excite a large number of Ru(phen)3 2+ to Ru(phen)3 2+* and exhibited a signal amplification due to its high quantum yield [44,45]. Consequently, the ECL intensity was greatly improved by ECL-RET in general.

Based on the above results, the proposed ECL aptasensor was applied for Pb2+ detection. Fig. 4 A illustrated the ECL response of the constructed aptasensor to different concentration of Pb2+. The ECL intensity decreased gradually with the increase of Pb2+ concentration. Moreover, the ECL decrement (ΔI ECL, the change in the ECL intensity in the presence and absence of target Pb2+) displayed a good linear relationship with the logarithmic value of Pb2+ concentration in the range of 0.1 pM to 1.0 nM (Fig. 4B) with a detection limit of 0.04 pM at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The analytical performance of the as-proposed ECL aptasensor in Pb2+ detection was compared with those of the previously reported methods (Table 1 ). The comparison indicated that the designed ECL aptasensor is one of the most excellent sensors for Pb2+ detection with desirable linear range and detection limit. The excellent performance benefited from the usage of PCNTs-AuNPs to suppress electrode passivation, ECL-RET promoted signal enhancement, and exonuclease-assisted target recycling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of this aptasensor, interferences study was performed by comparing the ΔI ECL value in presence of Pb2+ and ten other different metal ions with the same concentration of 1.0 nM. As shown in Fig. 4C, by comparison with Pb2+, all interfering ions resulted in negligible change in the ECL response, indicating that the proposed ECL aptasensor possessed good selectivity and specificity for Pb2+ detection.

The stability was assessed by calculating the response of the ECL sensor under 11 cycles of consecutive potential scans between 0 and −1.2 V (Fig. 4D). The obtained ECL signals were stable with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.36%, indicating that the proposed ECL sensor had satisfactory stability and reliability.

The reproducibility of the proposed ECL sensor was evaluated with five sensors fabricated with five different GCEs via the same modification steps independently. All electrodes showed similar ECL response and the relative standard deviation (RSD) toward 1.0 nM Pb2+ was 3.5% (Fig. S14), demonstrating the good reproducibility of the aptasensor.

To demonstrate the practicality of the proposed aptasensor, standard addition method was used to evaluate Pb2+ in tap water and lake water. The water samples were firstly pretreated by centrifugation and filtration, and spiked with different concentrations of Pb2+ standards. Then the spiked samples were diluted by 100-fold with detection solution. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of Pb2+ in these water samples were in the range of 96.5–106.8% (RSD from 2.4% to 4.7%). The results suggested that the proposed sensor was able to detect low concentrations of Pb2+ in real samples with good accuracy and reliability.

A novel ECL aptasensor was developed for sensitive detection of Pb2+ based on ECL of CNNFs which were prepared via a facile two-step hydrolysis-electrolysis strategy. The as-prepared CNNFs demonstrated well-preserved crystal structure and prominent PL and ECL properties. The aptasensor performed satisfactory sensitivity for Pb2+ detection thanks to the triple-amplification including the usage of PCNTs-AuNPs as electrocatalyst for ECL reaction and inhibitor of electrode passivation, ECL-RET from CNNFs to Ru(phen)3 2+, and exonuclease-assisted target recycling. This research would open an avenue for universal detection of other targets via simple aptamer replacement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405078, 21335004, and 21427807) and The Natural Science Foundation of Higher Education Institutions of Jiangsu Province (16KJB150017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.07.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent nucleic acid based biosensors for Pb2+ detection
A label-free GR-5DNAzyme sensor for lead ions detection based on nanoporous gold and anionic intercalator
Highly sensitive electrochemical sensing platform for lead ion based on synergetic catalysis of DNAzyme and Au-Pd porous bimetallic nanostructures
Dendritic structure DNA for specific metal ion biosensor based on catalytic hairpin assembly and a sensitive synergistic amplification strategy
Single-labeled hairpin probe for highly specific and sensitive detection of lead (II) based on the fluorescence quenching of deoxyguanosine and G-quartet
ZnO nanoflower-based photoelectrochemical DNAzyme sensor for the detection of Pb2+
Electrochemiluminescent resonance energy transfer of polymer dots for aptasensing
Sensitive electrochemiluminescent immunosensor for diabetic nephropathy analysis based on tris(bipyridine) ruthenium(II) derivative with binary intramolecular self-catalyzed property
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence from silicon nanocrystal quantum dots
Bimodal electrochemiluminescence of G-CNQDs in the presence of double coreactants for ascorbic acid detection
Highly sensitive and quality self-testable electrochemiluminescence assay of DNA methyltransferase activity using multifunctional sandwich-assembled carbon nitride nanosheets
Carbon dots/g-C3N4 nanoheterostructures-based signal-generation tags for photoelectrochemical immunoassay of cancer biomarkers coupling with copper nanoclusters
An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescent immunosensor based on graphene oxide coupled graphite-like carbon nitride and multiwalled carbon nanotubes-gold for the detection of diclofenac
Ultrathin graphitic C3N4 nanofibers: hydrolysis-driven top-down rapid synthesis and application as a novel fluorosensor for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of Fe3+
A fluorescent carbon nitride nanofibrous hydrogel for selective sensing of Cu2+
Synthesis and photocatalytic properties of graphitic carbon nitride nanofibers using porous anodic alumina templates
Facile gel-based morphological control of Ag/g-C3N4 porous nanofibers for photocatalytic hydrogen generation
Graphitic carbon nitride/nitrogen‐rich carbon nanofibers: highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution without cocatalysts
Zinc-doping enhanced cadmium sulfide electrochemiluminescence behavior based on Au-Cu alloy nanocrystals quenching for insulin detection
Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for microRNA detection by 3D DNA walking machine based target conversion and distance-controllable signal quenching and enhancing
Label-free electrochemiluminescent detection of transcription factors with hybridization chain reaction amplification
Determination of zinc ion based on electrochemiluminescence of Ru(phen)3 2+ and phenanthroline
Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescent determination of perphenazine at tris(1,10-phenanthroline)ruthenium(II)/nafion bulk modified carbon nanotube ceramic electrode via solid-phase microextraction
Proximity hybridization-regulated immunoassay for cell surface protein and protein-overexpressing cancer cells via electrochemiluminescence
A triple-amplification SPR electrochemiluminescence assay for chloramphenicol based on polymer enzyme-linked nanotracers and exonuclease-assisted target recycling
Signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence system coupled with target recycling amplification strategy for sensitive mercury ion and mucin 1 assay
Novel photoelectrochemical immunosensor for disease-related protein assisted by hemin/G-quadruplex-based DNAzyme on gold nanoparticles to enhance cathodic photocurrent on p-CuBi2O4 semiconductor
Activation of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) by alkaline hydrothermal treatment for photocatalytic NO oxidation in gas phase
